titleStmt.title: Andreev and normal reflections in gapped bilayer graphene-superconductor junctions

affiliation.orgName: Institute for Quantum Materials and Technologies
Institute for Quantum Materials and Technologies

analytic.title: Andreev and normal reflections in gapped bilayer graphene-superconductor junctions

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We study the Andreev and normal reflection processes-retro as well as specular-in a bilayer graphene-superconductor junction where equal and opposite displacement fields are applied for the top and bottom layers to induce a band gap. By employing the Dirac-Bogoliubov-de Gennes equation for the gapped bilayer graphene-superconductor junction, we calculate the reflections probabilities within the scattering theory approach. The subgap conductance, calculated in the framework of Blonder-Tinkham-Klapwijk formalism, shows the contribution from the Andreev retro-reflection (specular-reflection) when the applied bias voltage is below (above) the Fermi energy. Notably, both retro and specular reflections are modified in the presence of the displacement field, and the retro-tospecular crossover gets amplified when the displacement field is relatively small. They can be further tuned to either specular or retro Andreev reflection by adjusting the Fermi energy. Furthermore, our study reveals the simultaneous existence of double Andreev reflections and double normal reflections when the displacement field becomes comparable to the interlayer coupling strength. The existence of the normal retro-reflection process in a bilayer graphene-superconductor junction is a new finding which shows a distinctive feature in the conductance that can be experimentally verified.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Andreev reflection (AR) is a scattering process that occurs at a normal-superconductor (NS) junction and is solely responsible for converting a dissipative normal current into a dissipationless supercurrent [1,2]. In this process, when an electron from the N side is incident at the junction with excitation energy (Оө) less than the superconducting gap (вҲҶ), it is reflected back as a hole in a retro-reflection manner and a charge 2e is transferred on S side as a Cooper pair [3]. The effect of retro Andreev reflection (RAR) on the current-voltage relation has been studied in the seminal paper of the so-called BTK theory [4,5]. One consequence of it is the existence of subgap conductance for bias voltage eV < вҲҶ which can attain twice the value of the normal state conductance for a perfect transparent junction. Experimentally, a direct effect of RAR has also been observed as a sign change of velocity upon reflection [6] and zero-bias anomaly [7].

Graphene is an interesting system which has attracted the attention of the condensed matter community, not only due to its distinctive transport properties [8][9][10][11], owing to the relativistic linear dispersion and vanishing density of states at the Dirac points, but also because of a new phenomenon of electron-hole conversion at the graphene-superconductor junction-specular Andreev reflection (SAR) [12,13]. The nature of AR at the junction is expected to change from retro to specular since the Fermi energy (E F ) in graphene is significantly lower than the conventional metals. More precisely, when the Fermi energy is close to the charge neutrality point (CNP), i.e. E F вҶ’ 0, the incident electron from the conduction band (above the CNP) is reflected (in a specular manner) as a hole from the valence band (below the CNP). This interband electron-hole conversion phenomenon is known as the SAR. The study of SAR [12,13] has prompted a surge of interest in the graphene-superconductor junction, and thereafter various theoretical works have been put forward [14][15][16][17][18][19]. But, the experimental studies of Andreev processes have been limited so far [20][21][22][23][24][25][26] and the observation of SAR in graphene has not been succeeded yet [27] as the Fermienergy fluctuation is ОҙE F > вҲҶ which prevents attaining the E F вҶ’ 0 limit.

In contrast, the bilayer graphene (BLG) is regarded as a more suitable system compared to the monolayer graphene for observing the SAR, due to its low ОҙE F fluctuation [28][29][30][31]. Indeed, an experiment has been performed [28] and the measured subgap differential conductance shows a small dip around the bias eV = E F when E F is tuned below вҲҶ. This characteristic feature has been accredited to a crossover from retro-to-specular Andreev reflection. However, the retro-to-specular crossover color plots show a very weak consent between the experiment and theory that are presented in Figs. 2 and3(a) in Ref. [28], see especially the different colorbar scales used for the experimental and theoretical data plots. This raises speculation regarding the observation of SAR in BLG. Moreover, a theoretical study to enhance the SAR contribution in subgap conductance has been suggested by introducing a Zeeman field on the normal side of the bilayer graphene NS junction [32].

In this paper, we propose an alternate study to enhance the retro-to-specular crossover in BLG by applying two different displacement fields. Without loss of generality, we consider that the generated electrostatic potentials, due to the displacement fields, are О» for the top layer and -О» for the bottom layer which creates a gap вҲј 2О» in the BLG band structure when О» вүӘ t вҠҘ , where t вҠҘ is being the interlayer coupling. Consequently, the subgap differential conductance across the NS junction vanishes and widens the retro-to-specular crossover, from a point at bias eV = E F to a whole range of bias |E F -О»| < eV < |E F + О»| with width 2О». This enhancement due to the displacement field would possibly help to observe the crossover experimentally as the boundary becomes wider. In addition, we also investigate the reflection processes and the corresponding differential conductance when the displacement field becomes large and comparable to the interlayer coupling, i.e., О» вүІ t вҠҘ . Interestingly, in this regime, four reflection processes exist simultaneously: specular normal reflection (SNR), retro normal reflection (RNR), specular Andreev reflection (SAR), and retro normal reflection (RAR). This occurs due to the partial inversion of the lower conduction/valence band upon the applied displacement field. The existence of RNR is a new finding in the system of bilayer graphene-superconductor junction which exhibits a distinctive characteristic feature in the differential conductance.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Sec. II, we present the effective low-energy Hamiltonian of the gapped bilayer graphene and the corresponding band structure. The model and formalism are illustrated in Sec. III, introducing the Dirac-Bogoliubov-de Gennes (DBdG) equation and the excitation energy eigenstates for the normal and superconducting sides of the junction. Sec. IV provides the results for the reflection probabilities and normalized conductance. They are discussed in detail separately for the small and large displacement fields in subsections IV A, IV B, and IV C, respectively. Sec. V concludes the paper.

div.head: II. GAPPED BILAYER GRAPHENE
div.p: The bilayer graphene comprises two layers of graphene, wherein two non-equivalent A 1 and B 1 carbon atoms in one layer and A 2 and B 2 in the other layer. The two layers are stacked either in A 1 -A 2 or in B 1 -A 2 structure [33,34]. A quantum Monte Carlo simulation suggests that the latter structure is more stable [35], so we consider the B 1 -A 2 stacking which is commonly known as the Bernal stacking [33], see in Fig. 1(i). Within the tight-binding approximation and considering only the first nearest-neighbour hopping in each layer and B 1 -A 2 interlayer hopping, the relevant low-energy Hamiltonian can be deduced in momentum-space [36][37][38][39][40][41][42] which reads in basis (ПҲ
div.formula: A1 ПҲ B1 ПҲ A2 ПҲ B2 ) T as H = пЈ« пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈӯ О» в„Ҹv F k - 0 0 в„Ҹv F k + О» -t вҠҘ 0 0 -t вҠҘ -О» в„Ҹv F k - 0 0 в„Ҹv F k + -О» пЈ¶ пЈ· пЈ· пЈё(1)
div.p: Here, k Вұ = k x Вұ iО·k y вүЎ -i(вҲӮ x Вұ iО·вҲӮ y ) are the wavevectors that are measured from the corners of 2D hexag-onal Brillouin zone's Dirac points K(K вҖІ ) for different valley О· = +(-); v F вүғ 10 6 m/s is the Fermi-velocity and в„Ҹ is the reduced Planck's constant. О» is added to account for the on-site potential (equal in magnitude but opposite in sign for the two layers) which can be tuned by different displacement fields. A schematic depiction to induce О» is shown in Fig. 1(ii). The non-zero О» creates a band gap in BLG band structure, and the gap can be tuned up to 250 meV [43][44][45][46][47][48][49]. We set в„Ҹv F = 1 for the calculation hereafter.

div.p: FIG. 1. Bernal-stacked bilayer graphene structure and a schematic depiction to induce the displacement field are in (i) and (ii), respectively. In (iii), the low-energy band structure of bilayer graphene, EОҪ,Вұ for ОҪ = Вұ in Eq. ( 2), with respect to k. The behavior of conduction bands (E+,Вұ) and valence bands (E-,Вұ) for the displacement field (a) О» = 0, (b) О» = 0.06t вҠҘ , and (c) О» = 0.6t вҠҘ . We observe a band gap opening and the Mexican-hat-like shape formation in E+,and E-,-as О» is increased.
The eigenvalues of H in Eq. ( 1) provide valley degenerated low-energy bands
div.formula: E ОҪ,Вұ = ОҪ k 2 + t 2 вҠҘ 2 + О» 2 Вұ t 2 вҠҘ 2 2 + k 2 (4О» 2 + t 2 вҠҘ )(2)
div.p: where k = k 2 x + k 2 y and the index ОҪ = +(-) labels the conduction (valence) bands. In Fig. 1(iii), we plot the conduction bands E +,Вұ (solid lines) and the valence bands E -,Вұ (dashed lines) as a function of k. The nature of BLG dispersion is quadratic, unlike the linear dispersion for monolayer graphene; the former is due to hopping between the layers, t вҠҘ . In absence of the displacement field, О» = 0, the lower conduction band E +,- and the upper valence band E -,-touch at the zero energy at k = 0 [see in Fig. 1(iii)(a)]; however, the bands E +,+ and E -,+ show a gap of Вұt вҠҘ . When О» is present, we notice a gap opening and Mexican-hat-like shape in E +,-and E -,-. The results are shown in Figs. 1(iii)(b)-(c). Analytical evaluation of the bands E Вұ,-provides three extremal points k I = 0 and k Вұ II = Вұ 4О» 4 +2(О»t вҠҘ ) 2 4О» 2 +t 2 вҠҘ which correspond to energies E Вұ,-= ВұО» and E Вұ,-= ВұО»t вҠҘ / 4О» 2 + t 2 вҠҘ , respectively. This leads to the band gaps 2О» at k I and 2О»t вҠҘ / 4О» 2 + t 2 вҠҘ at k Вұ II . Notably, when О» вүӘ t вҠҘ , the two gaps approximately become equal for k I and k Вұ II as can be seen in Fig. 1(iii)(b). In contrast, they are different when О» is comparable to t вҠҘ , i.e. О» вүІ t вҠҘ , see in Fig. 1(iii)(c). The minimum and maximum of E +,-are respectively E min = О»t вҠҘ / 4О» 2 + t 2 вҠҘ and E max = О».

div.head: III. MODEL AND FORMALISM
div.p: In order to investigate the scattering processes and the transport properties, we consider an NS junction on the gapped BLG sheet formed at x = 0 in x-y plane and assume that x < 0 is the region N, while x > 0 occupies the region S. The superconductivity in the S region can be induced through the proximity effect by covering an external s-wave superconducting electrode [12,50]. We employ the Dirac-Bogoliubov-de Gennes (DBdG) equation [12,51] which couples electron with the timereversed hole excitation wavefunctions via the superconducting (SC) pair potential. It reads
div.formula: H -E F вҲҶ(r) вҲҶ(r) E F -T HT -1 u e v h = Оө u e v h(3)
div.p: where Оө вүҘ 0 is the excitation energy, u e (v h ) is the electron (hole) spinor wavefunction, and T denotes time-reversal operator. We consider the SC gap to be uniform with вҲҶ(r) = вҲҶОҳ(x) where r = (x, y) and Оҳ(x) is the Heaviside step function. Also, H = diag(H + , H -) with H Вұ = H Вұ + U (r) where H Вұ are given in Eq. ( 1) for the valley О· = Вұ and U (r) = -U 0 Оҳ(x) is a doping potential in S region. For U 0 вү« E F , the Fermi wavelength on the N side is much larger compared to the S side which enables вҲҶ(r) to attain its bulk value вҲҶ. In the absence of magnetic field, the Eq. ( 3) preserves the time-reversal symmetry such that T HT -1 = H. However, this operation interchanges the valley K to K вҖІ and vice-versa. So, we can decouple the Eq. (3) into two sets of eight equations,
div.formula: H О· -E F вҲҶ(r) вҲҶ(r) E F -H О· u e v h = Оө u e v h .(4)
div.p: Notice that the subscript О· in the above equation serves another purpose; the DBdG Eq. ( 4) for О· = +(-) cou-ples electron excitation from the valley K(K вҖІ ) to hole excitation at the valley K вҖІ (K).

To find the excitation energy spectrum for Eq. ( 4) on both sides of the junction, we follow the similar calculation procedures as given in Ref. [12] and consider a plane-wave solution (u e v h ) T e ikxx+ikyy . For the N side, the excitation energies for electron (e) and hole (h) are
div.formula: Оө e ОҪ,Вұ = E ОҪ,Вұ -E F and Оө h ОҪ,Вұ = E F -E ОҪ,Вұ(5)
div.p: Similarly, for the S side, the excitation energies for electron-like and hole-like quasiparticles are Оө (S,1)
div.formula: Вұ,Вұ = Вұ [(U 0 + E F ) Вұ Оі 1 ] 2 + вҲҶ 2 (6a) Оө (S,2) Вұ,Вұ = Вұ [(U 0 + E F ) Вұ Оі 2 ] 2 + вҲҶ 2(6b)
div.p: where
div.formula: Оі i = k 2 + t 2 вҠҘ 2 + О» 2 + (-1) i t 2 вҠҘ 2 2 + k 2 (4О» 2 + t 2 вҠҘ ) for i = 1, 2.
div.p: Since the system is translationally invariant along the y-direction, the transverse momentum k y is conserved during the scattering process. Therefore, for a given Оө and k y , we solve the Eq. ( 4) on the N side to obtain the state vector and longitudinal momentum k x . The О·-dependent states for electron and hole are

div.p: However, for S region, finding an explicit analytical expression for the state vector is a substantially difficult task as the nonzero вҲҶ couples u e and v h . Additionally, the matrix size of the DBdG Eq. ( 4) for the gapped BLG is double in comparison to the monolayer graphene [12]. Therefore, the state vector in this region is calculated numerically, say, u О· S (Оө, k x ). Nevertheless, we deduce an analytical form for longitudinal momenta: {Вұk (S,1)
x,Вұ , Вұk
div.formula: (S,2) x,Вұ } with k (S,i) x,Вұ = О“ 2 i + О» 2 -k 2 y Вұ (4О» 2 + t 2 вҠҘ )О“ 2 i -(О»t вҠҘ ) 2 where О“ i = (U 0 + E F ) -(-1) i вҲҡ Оө 2 -вҲҶ 2 for i = 1, 2.
div.p: Using these states, we construct the scattering wavefunctions for both sides which consist Andreev and normal reflection coefficients and transmission coefficients, and that are obtained by demanding the continuity of the wavefunctions at the junction x = 0. By utilizing these coefficients, we calculate the Andreev and normal reflections probabilities and the differential conductance in the framework of Blonder-Tinkham-Klapwijk (BTK) formalism [4]. See the Appendix A and C for the technical details that are given for the two regimes О» вүӘ t вҠҘ and О» вүІ t вҠҘ , respectively.

div.p: q s j a Z + H a P l L f p t 5 l R V v o J 9 n 6 9 B u P P P o m K 5 V 0 m K a / e t H G k 6 f P N r e e x y 9 e v n q 9 3 d 9 < l a t e x i t s h a 1 _ b a s e 6 4 = " n j o q h V U 8 In Fig. 2, we have schematically shown the possible reflection processes involved in both regimes. For О» вүӘ t вҠҘ regime, the Figs. 2(a)-(b) show the usual RAR, SAR, and SNR reflections and the involved excitation energy contours for electron and hole that participate in the scattering process. As О» is small, it only opens a band gap вҲј 2О» [see in Fig. 1(iii)(b)] and shows the usual reflections [28,30]. However, for О» вүІ t вҠҘ regime in Figs. 2(c)-(d), four reflections happen simultaneously which are the RAR, SAR, SNR, and RNR [52]. The extra RNR process occurs because of the inversion of the lower conduction band [see in Fig. 1(iii)(c)] for large О». This supports two isoenergy-contours for electron excitation Оө e +,-and two for hole excitation Оө h +,-when Fermi energy E F is set in-between the maximum and minimum of the lower conduction band. Consequently, the SNR and RNR from Оө e +,-and the RAR and SAR from Оө h +,-occur, which are shown in Fig. 2(d).

div.p: In the next section, we present the results for the reflection probabilities and differential conductance at zero temperature and discuss them in detail. They are calculated by using the Eqs. (A2), (A3), (C2), and (C3). For numerical calculation, we fix вҲҶ = 1 and set all energy parameters in units of вҲҶ. Since the bulk SC pair potential can be achieved вҲҶ вҲј 1.2 meV (by depositing NbSe 2 on the BLG sheet [28]) and the interlayer coupling in BLG is roughly t вҠҘ вҲј 0.39 eV, we present all results for t вҠҘ = 400вҲҶ, except in Figs. 4(c IV. RESULTS

div.head: A. Small displacement field, О» вүӘ t вҠҘ
div.p: In this subsection, we work in regime (О», E F , Оө, вҲҶ) вүӘ t вҠҘ which set the longitudinal momenta for П„ =mode k e- x and k h- x imaginary, and consequently the corresponding state vectors in Eqs. ( 7) and ( 8) become evanescent type. So, we consider only П„ = + mode for the incident electron which governs the scattering mechanism. The possible reflection processes are depicted in Figs. 2(a) and 2(b), and the reflection probabilities and differential conductance formula are given in Appendix A.

div.head: Reflection probabilities
div.p: We present the normal and Andreev reflection probabilities, R О· n,+ and R О· a,+ , with respect to the excitation energy Оө and incident angle Оұ. Figure 3 shows R О· n,+ and R О· a,+ plots at valley О· = K(K вҖІ ) for parameters E F = 0.5вҲҶ and U 0 = 10вҲҶ. In Figs. 3(a)-(b), we set the displacement field О» = 0, while it is set О» = 0.1вҲҶ in Figs. 3(c)-(f). For the excitation range 0 < Оө < 1.5вҲҶ, the scattering process involves bands Оө e +,-, Оө h +,-, and Оө h -,-from the N side; however, only band Оө (S,1) +,-is involved from the S side. The incident electron always come from Оө e +,-and the reflected hole belongs to Оө h +,for 0 < Оө < (E FО») whereas it comes from Оө h -,-for Оө > (E F + О»). When Оө > вҲҶ, the transmitted quasiparticles (electron-like as well as hole-like) belong to Оө (S,1) +,-.
In Figs. 3(a
div.formula: )-(b), R K(K вҖІ ) n,+ and R K(K вҖІ ) a,+
div.p: are valley degenerated and symmetric with respect to Оұ. When 0 < Оө < E F , at a given Оө, the R О· n,+ is enhanced while R О· a,+ is weakened as Оұ increases from 0 to ПҖ/2. The R О· n,+ (R О· a,+ ) for E F < Оө < вҲҶ starts approximately equal to 1(0) [see the colorbar of Fig. 3] at Оұ = 0 and decreases (increases) with Оұ/ПҖ вүІ 0.25 and again reaches to 1(0) for further increasing. We notice that R О· n,+ + R О· a,+ = 1 as no transmissions are allowed in subgap (Оө < вҲҶ) region, while it is weakened R О· n,+ + R О· a,+ Мё = 1 in Оө > вҲҶ region because the quasiparticles transmission also happen. When О» = 0.1вҲҶ in Figs. 3(c)-(f), both R О· n,+ and R О· a,+ follow similar behaviour as in Figs. 3(a)-(b), but they are now slightly asymmetric about Оұ for each valley О· = K, K вҖІ . Changing the valley from K вҶ’ K вҖІ , this asymmetry is reversed, i.e.
div.formula: R K n,+ (Оұ) = R K вҖІ n,+ (-Оұ) and R K a,+ (Оұ) = R K вҖІ a,+ (-Оұ)
div.p: , which is a direct consequence of the layer asymmetry as О» is nonzero. The presence of О» opens a gap вҲј 2О» at Оө = E F (cyan dashed-line) for the incident angle Оұ = 0 as neither Оө h +,-nor Оө h -,-is available for the excitation range (E FО») < Оө < (E F + О»), and as a result, Andreev reflection vanishes R О· a,+ = 0 and normal reflection reaches to R О· n,+ = 1.

div.head: Differential conductance
div.p: Figure 4 shows the normalized conductance G/G 0 versus applied bias voltage Оө = eV when О» = 0 which is obtained by using Eq. (A3). First, we discuss the result in the limit t вҠҘ вү« U 0 in Figs. 4(a)-(b) for t вҠҘ = 400вҲҶ and U 0 = 10вҲҶ. This limit corresponds to the work in Ref. [29] where authors calculated the subgap conductance approximately. As discussed in the previous subsection, the scattering process always involves Оө e +,-for electron, and Оө h +,-for the reflected hole when eV < E F (RAR) else Оө h -,-when eV > E F (SAR), whereas Оө (S,1) +,-accounts for the quasiparticles transmission when eV > вҲҶ. The G/G0 for several fixed EF in limit t вҠҘ вү« U0. (b) The effect of U0 on G/G0 for EF = 0.5вҲҶ. (c) In opposite limit t вҠҘ вүӘ U0, a dip in G/G0 is observed near the gap eV вҲј вҲҶ when EF < вҲҶ. (d) For EF = 0, the dip near the SC gap moves closure to вҲҶ as the interlayer coupling t вҠҘ increases.

In Fig. 4(a), we plot G/G 0 for several fixed E F . For E F < вҲҶ, it begins with a finite value and starts decreasing as eV increases and vanishes at eV = E F since no Andreev reflection happens at any incident angle. On further increasing eV , it again rises, exhibiting a singularity at eV = вҲҶ similar to the ordinary NS junction [4]. The G/G 0 becomes weak when eV > вҲҶ, due to the quasiparticles transmission. However, for E F вүҘ вҲҶ, only RAR contributes to the subgap conductance and reaches G/G 0 вҶ’ 2 for large E F , see the curve for E F = 10вҲҶ. The Fermi energy mismatch at the junction, due to the finite doping potential U 0 = 10вҲҶ, prevents achieving the maximum value of G/G 0 = 2. In Fig. 4(b), we show U 0 -dependent G/G 0 at E F = 0.5вҲҶ. It shows a strong suppression on increasing U 0 as the Fermi energy mismatch at the junction increases with U 0 which reduces the AR, leading to the suppression. Our results are numerically exact and are in agreement with the findings in Ref. [29].

Next, we set the parameters t вҠҘ = 10вҲҶ and U 0 = 400вҲҶ and work in the opposite limit t вҠҘ вүӘ U 0 to observe the dip in G/G 0 near the gap eV вҲј вҲҶ which was attributed to the pseudospin-1 effect in Ref. [30]. Similar to the case in Figs. 4(a)-(b), the scattering process for t вҠҘ вүӘ U 0 involves the bands Оө e +,-and Оө h +,-(Оө h -,-) for electron and reflected hole, but the quasiparticles now participate from bands Оө (S,1) +,-and Оө (S,2) +,-for the transmission. Consequently, we observe a dip in the conductance near вҲҶ, see in Figs. 4(c)-(d). The G/G 0 plots for E F = 0, 0.5вҲҶ, and 10вҲҶ in Fig. 4(c) and for t вҠҘ = 3вҲҶ and 10вҲҶ in Fig. 4(d) are generated for the same values of parameters taken in [30]. We obtain exactly the same results for G/G 0 as obtained in Ref. [30]. This shows that our numerical method for the bilayer graphene-superconductor junction produces exact results, and also works in the both limits t вҠҘ вү« U 0 and t вҠҘ вүӘ U 0 . 5. Normalized conductance G/G0 with respect to eV /вҲҶ at fixed EF for different О». The top panel (a) is for the limit t вҠҘ вү« U0 whereas the bottom panel (b) corresponds to the limit t вҠҘ вүӘ U0. The G/G0 vanishes for the bias voltage range (EF -О») < eV < (EF + О») with gap width 2О» and shows a strong modification in the presence of displacement field. Now, we present the normalized conductance G/G 0 behaviour in the presence of displacement field О». The results are plotted in Fig. 5(a) for the limit t вҠҘ вү« U 0 and in Fig. 5(b) for the limit t вҠҘ вүӘ U 0 . They are shown for different О» at E F = 0.5вҲҶ. We restrict О» вүӨ E F . As can be seen that the G/G 0 vanishes around E F for the bias range (E FО») < eV < (E F + О») at any finite О», creating a gap of width вҲј 2О» (solid lines) along the applied bias voltage axis. It happens because no Andreev reflections (neither RAR nor SAR) take place in this range as both the hole bands Оө h +,-and Оө h -,-are absent. This strong modification due to the finite displacement field О» suggests that we can tune the subgap conductance and amplify the retro-to-specular crossover which would possibly help to realise the crossover boundary experimentally [28], since the crossover-boundary becomes wider.
div.formula: G/G 0 (b) t вҠҘ = 10вҲҶ, U 0 = 400вҲҶ E F = 0.5вҲҶ FIG.
B. Large displacement field, О» вүІ t вҠҘ
div.p: This section is devoted to studying the transport properties at the junction in large displacement field regime, wherein the band structure illustrated in Fig. 1(iii)(c) participates in the scattering process. In this regime, both the П„ = Вұ modes with incident electron longitudinal wave-vectors k eВұ x are present. The possible reflection processes are schematically shown in Figs. 2(c) and 2(d).

We fix E F comparable to О», i.e. E F вүғ О», and set the parameters t вҠҘ = 400вҲҶ, E F = 80вҲҶ, and U 0 = 10вҲҶ for the calculation. We first calculate the reflection probabilities at a given О» with respect to Оұ and Оө. Using these probabilities, the conductance is calculated by integrating over Оұ, see the formulation and technical details in Appendix C. For brevity, we only present the results for differential conductance and discuss the key findings (the Оұ-dependent and Оө-dependent reflection probabilities are presented in Appendix C for completeness).

Figure 6(a) presents the normalized conductance G/G 0 versus eV /вҲҶ for several values of О» taken around E F . The scattering process involves only the conduction band excitations Оө e +,-and Оө h +,-for electron and hole, respectively. So, the electron-hole conversion for the Andreev reflections is always intraband in nature. To better understand the behavior of G/G 0 , we also plot quantities Оҙ min = E F -E min and Оҙ max = E max -E F with respect to О» in Fig. 6(b), which are depicted in the bottom panel of Fig. 6(c). The filled and open circles, in Fig. 6(b), are at the selected О» points for which G/G 0 is plotted. The top panel of Fig. 6(c) shows the situation when E F lies above E max in the conduction band, i.e, E F > E max .

For О» = 78вҲҶ, in Fig. 6(a), only the П„ = + mode wave-vectors are available for electron and hole excitations for bias 0 < eV < 1.6вҲҶ, so the SAR and RNR vanish as the П„ =mode is absent due to the condition E F > E max . Therefore, only the RAR and SNR contribute to the conductance. G/G 0 вҶ’ 2 in the subgap region remains almost constant because E F вү« вҲҶ and provides a similar result as shown in Fig. 4(a E F = 10вҲҶ and behaves as an ordinary NS junction [4]. When О» = 80вҲҶ(= E F ), the scattering process is still governed by П„ = + mode wave-vectors for electron excitation and thus similar G/G 0 behavior, but now both Andreev reflections (RAR and SAR) exist along with SNR due to the presence of П„ = Вұ wave-vectors for hole excitation. Next, we notice that the G/G 0 starts with a lower value and rises again to the previous value at the critical bias (eV ) c = 0.5вҲҶ for О» = 80.5вҲҶ (dotted green curve, Оҙ max = 0.5вҲҶ) and (eV ) c = вҲҶ for О» = 81вҲҶ (dot-dashed purple curve, Оҙ max = вҲҶ) because the П„ = Вұ modes participate for electron and hole in range 0 < eV < (eV ) c . This leads to the appearance of a new type of normal reflection-retro normal reflection (RNR). Correspondingly, it weakens G/G 0 in the region 0 < eV < (eV ) c .

div.p: In contrast, when О» > (E F + вҲҶ), both П„ = Вұ modes wave-vectors from Оө e +,-are always available for electron excitation, but depending on the strength of О» the hole excitation Оө h +,-does not always participate in the scattering process for the whole range of eV . For instance, Оө h +,-is always involved at О» = 82вҲҶ and 84вҲҶ, and thus all four reflections contribute. The further decrease in G/G within the subgap region is due to the presence of RNR along with SNR, whereas the quasiparticle transmissions are responsible for the decrease in the eV > вҲҶ region. However, the hole excitation Оө h +,-is absent when bias voltage exceeds the critical bias (eV ) c = вҲҶ, 0.61вҲҶ, and 0.22вҲҶ for the displacement fields О» = 86вҲҶ (Оҙ min = вҲҶ), О» = 86.5вҲҶ (Оҙ min = 0.61вҲҶ), and О» = 87вҲҶ (Оҙ min = 0.22вҲҶ), respectively. As a result, the double Andreev reflections SAR and RAR become zero, and the non-zero double normal reflections SNR and RNR cause the zero conductance inside the gap for (eV ) c < eV < вҲҶ. These results are consistent with the discussion of the reflection probabilities, given in Appendix C. Overall, we observe that G/G 0 clearly shows a distinct feature which could be experimentally verified by varying the displacement field close to the Fermi energy of the normal side.
C. Experimentally-feasible results: О» = 0 (in S region)

In the preceding two subsections, we examined the conductance characteristics in the small and large displacement field regimes while keeping О» non-zero and equal on both the N and S sides of the junction. Nevertheless, implementing a non-zero displacement field on the S side in an experimental setup would likely pose considerable challenges, if not impossibilities. Therefore, in this subsection, we focus on calculating the conductance in the small and large displacement field regimes when О» is turned off on the S side; however, it is still applied on the N side.

In Fig. 7, we show the normalized conductance G/G with respect to eV /вҲҶ for different values of О» in the regime О» вүӘ t вҠҘ . The other fixed parameters are given in the figure panels and caption. Notice that the conductance curves in Fig. 7(a) exhibit almost exactly the same result as in Fig. 5(a) even though О» = 0 in the S region. This happens mainly because the Fermi energy mismatch at the junction does not change significantly since О» вүӘ U 0 as U 0 = 10вҲҶ in the both calculation. In contrast, this difference is visible when we set U 0 = вҲҶ in Fig. 7(a) as О» вҲј U 0 . However, the qualitative behaviour is still similar. Now, we present the G/G 0 behaviour in the large displacement field limit, see Fig. 8, for the same set of parameters values as in Fig. 6, except here the displacement field О» is non-zero only on N side, i.e., О» N = О» and О» S = 0. We see that the qualitative characteristics of G/G 0 is similar to the Fig. 6(a), but now the subgap conductance has dropped significantly as the Fermi-energy mismatch is enlarged because of О» S = 0. Consequently, the Andreev reflections (normal reflections) contribution to the conductance would be decreased (increased), reducing the subgap conductance.

div.head: V. CONCLUSION
div.p: We have studied the scattering reflection processes at the gapped bilayer graphene-superconducting junction by employing the DBdG equation within the scattering theory approach. Since О» provides an external tool to tune the BLG band structure, we have thoroughly investigated its effect on the normal reflections, the Andreev reflections, and the experimentally accessible quantity the differential conductance in two regimes: О» вүӘ t вҠҘ and О» вүІ t вҠҘ . In the former regime, we have revisited the О» = 0 case first and obtained the expected specular (retro) Andreev reflection for the excitation energy Оө > E F (Оө < E F ); the retro-to-specular crossing point at bias eV = E F in the subgap conductance [29,30]. As our numerical calculation provides the exact result, we also observe the dip in conductance near eV вҲј вҲҶ when t вҠҘ вүӘ U 0 , which was attributed to the pseudospin-1 feature in Ref. [30]. However, this dip does not appear when t вҠҘ вү« U 0 , as is also the case in Ref. [29]. Furthermore, introducing a non-zero small О» broadens the retro-to-specular crossing for the bias range |E F -О»| < eV < |E F + О»| in the subgap conductance which is a direct manifestation of the band gap between conduction and valence bands due to finite displacement field. This suggests that tuning О» the crossover region can be easily modified and possibly obtained experimentally as the authors in [28] struggle to observe it. However, for the latter regime О» вүІ t вҠҘ , we have shown that apart from SNR, SAR, and RAR, there exists also RNR due to the presence of the Mexican-hat-shape band structure in the gapped BLG. This is a new finding in a bilayer graphene NS junction which has never been explored as per our knowledge. The normalized conductance shows a very distinct characteristic feature when all four reflections contribute, see Fig. 6, and it can be distinguished from the result when the Mexican-hat structure does not affect the scattering process.

div.p: Here, k e- x (k h- x ) = -iОә e- x (-iОә h- x ) and k S x,j for j = 1, ..., 4, are the wave-vectors of side S and are chosen appropriately from {Вұk (S,1)
x,Вұ , Вұk (S,2)

x,Вұ }. The coefficients r О· n,Вұ , r О· a,Вұ , and t j=1,..,4 are normal reflections, Andreev reflections, and transmissions, respectively. r О· n,-and r О· a,-are always zero because of evanescent solutions for the П„ =mode. It is worth mentioning that r О· n,+ is always SNR, but r О· a,+ is RAR for Оө < (E FО»), ОІ = 1 and SAR for Оө > (E F + О»), ОІ = -1. By using the continuity condition, ОЁ О· | x=0 -= ОЁ О· | x=0 + , we obtain these coefficients for a given Оө and incident angle Оұ defined from k + y = (E F + Оө) 2 + О» 2 + ОЈ e sin Оұ where Оұ вҲҲ [-ПҖ/2, ПҖ/2]. So, the reflection probabilities are obtained as:
div.formula: R О· n,+ = |r О· n,+ (Оө, Оұ)| 2 , R О· a,+ = V О· h+ V О· e+ |r О· a,+ (Оө, Оұ)| 2 (A2) with velocities V О· e+ = вҹЁu О· e (Оө, k e+ x )| вҲӮHО· вҲӮkx |u О· e (Оө, k e+ x )вҹ© and V О· h+ = вҹЁv О· h (Оө, k h+ x )| вҲӮHО· вҲӮkx |v О· h (Оө, k h+ x )вҹ©.
div.p: The differential conductance within the BTK framework [4] at zero temperature can be expressed as follows:
div.formula: G(Оө) = О· g 0 (Оө) ПҖ 2 -ПҖ 2 1 -R О· n,+ + R О· a,+ cos Оұ dОұ (A3) with Оө = eV and g 0 (Оө) = 2e 2 W hПҖ (E F + Оө) 2 + О» 2 + ОЈ e
div.p: where W is the width of BLG sheet and factor 2 accounts for the spin degeneracy. Also, the differential conductance for a normal-to-normal BLG junction is G 0 (Оө) = 4g 0 (Оө) where the factor 4 is due to sum of valley index О· = Вұ and incident angle 29,30]. In this regime, we have both the П„ = Вұ modes for the incident electron with the wave-vectors (k e+ x , k + y ) and (-k e- x , k - y ) where k Вұ y = (E F + Оө) 2 + О» 2 Вұ ОЈ e sin Оұ. So, the scattering wavefunctions on both sides of the junction have the form ОЁ О· П„ (x)e ik П„ y y with

ОЁ О· П„ (x) = u О· e (Оө, П„ k eП„ x )e iП„ k eП„ x x + r О· n1,П„ u О· e (Оө, -k e+ x )e -ik e+ x x + r О· n2,П„ u О· e (Оө, k e- x )e ik e- x x 1 0 + r О· a1,П„ v О· h (Оө, -k h- x )e -ik h- x x + r О· a2,П„ v О· h (Оө, k h+ x )e ik h+ x x 0 1 , for x вүӨ 0 = 4 j=1 t О· j,П„ u О· S (Оө, k S x,j,П„ )e ik S x,j,П„ x , for x вүҘ 0 (C1)
div.p: where r О· n1,П„ , r О· n2,П„ , r О· a1,П„ , and r О· a2,П„ denote reflection coefficients, namely, SNR, RNR, SAR, and RAR for П„ = + mode; however, they become RNR, SNR, RAR, and SAR for П„ =mode. The t О· j,П„ for j = 1, ..., 4 are transmission coefficients. Again, using the continuity condition, we obtain the reflection probabilities as:

div.head: Оұ-dependent normal and Andreev reflections
div.p: We show the reflection probabilities as a function of Оұ at three Оө = 0.3вҲҶ, 0.8вҲҶ, and 1.2вҲҶ for О» = 84вҲҶ and 86.5вҲҶ in Figs. 10(a)-(c) and 10(d)-(f), respectively. Since О» is non-zero, these probabilities are slightly asymmetric about Оұ. When О» = 84вҲҶ, the electron and hole excitations span Оө e +,-вҲҲ [-2.55вҲҶ, вҲһ] and Оө h +,-вҲҲ [-вҲһ, 2.55вҲҶ]; the double normal reflections (SNR and RNR) and double Andreev reflections (SAR and RAR) exist for the intersection of -2.55вҲҶ вүІ Оө e +,-< 4вҲҶ and -4вҲҶ < Оө h +,-вүІ 2.55вҲҶ. All four probabilities are non-zero only around Оұ = 0 at Оө = 0.3вҲҶ and 0.8вҲҶ. The RAR R K a2,+ is dominating the scattering process. As Оұ is increased from 0 to ПҖ/2, the R K a2,+ enhances first, reaching to R K a2,+ вүҲ 1, and then starts decreasing and eventually goes to zero, while RNR R K n2,+ and SAR R K a1,+ weaken and slowly vanish, see the results in Figs. 10(a) and 10(b). Moreover, the SNR R K n1,+ steadily decreases and almost vanishes before it strikingly rises to the value 1. In Fig. 10(c) at Оө = 1.2вҲҶ, the magnitude of all reflections is weakened because of the quasiparticles transmission for Оө > вҲҶ. Notice that the sum of all reflection probabilities equals 1 for Оө < вҲҶ and becomes less than 1 for Оө > вҲҶ.

For О» = 86.5вҲҶ, in Figs. 10(d)-(f), electron and hole excitations now span Оө e +,-вҲҲ [-0.61вҲҶ, вҲһ] and Оө h +,-вҲҲ [-вҲһ, 0.61вҲҶ], and all the four probabilities exist only for the intersection of -0.61вҲҶ вүІ Оө e +,-< 6.5вҲҶ and -6.5вҲҶ < Оө h +,-вүІ 0.61вҲҶ, see the results in Fig. 10(d) at Оө = 0.3вҲҶ. Interestingly, RAR R K a2,+ is suppressed while RNR R K n2,+ is now dominating the scattering process around Оұ = 0. For Оө > 0.61вҲҶ in Figs. 10(e) and 10(f), the hole excitation Оө h +,-is absent so the double Andreev reflections SAR R K a1,+ and RAR R K a2,+ vanish, but the double normal reflections SNR R K n1,+ and RNR R K n2,+ still exist as the electron excitation Оө e +,-is always present.

div.head: Оө-dependent normal and Andreev reflections
div.p: Here, we discuss these probabilities versus Оө, see in Fig. 11, at three incident angles Оұ = 0, 0.25ПҖ, and 0.4ПҖ. When О» = 84вҲҶ, both Оө e +,-and Оө h +,-are present for whole range 0 < Оө < 1.5вҲҶ therefore all four reflection probabilities are present at Оұ = 0 [see Fig. 11(a)]. The SNR R K n1,+ , SAR R K a1,+ , and RAR R K a2,+ are slightly enhanced on increasing Оө, and then they are suppressed after Оө > вҲҶ due to the quasiparticles transmission, whereas the RNR R K n2,+ is opposite in nature. At Оұ = 0.25ПҖ in Fig. 11(b), the RAR starts with R K a2,+ = 1 as other reflec-tions are zero, and it gradually decreases due to small rise in SNR around Оө = вҲҶ and then falls rapidly. However, at Оұ = 0.4ПҖ, RNR and SAR are always zero while RAR (SNR) decreases (increases) with Оө but RAR vanishes for Оө < вҲҶ and SNR attains unity, see Fig. 11(c). The critical value of Оө at which RAR vanishes is Оө c вүҲ 0.58вҲҶ as the critical angle for retro-reflected hole becomes Оұ c вүЎ arcsin (k h+ /k e+ ) = 0.4ПҖ where k h+ and k e+ are the magnitude of hole and electron momenta.

When we set О» = 86.5вҲҶ, the hole excitation Оө h +,-is absent for Оө > 0.61вҲҶ, and consequently, both Andreev reflections SAR and RAR are zero. However, they are non-zero for Оө < 0.61вҲҶ and are decreasing slowly as Оө is increased, see in Fig. 11(d) for Оұ = 0. Concerning the double normal reflections, they are non-zero and show non-trivial behaviour. The Fig. 11(e) for Оұ = 0.25ПҖ shows similar results except RNR vanishes before Andreev reflections become zero and SNR attain unity for 0.61вҲҶ < Оө < вҲҶ. For Оұ = 0.4ПҖ in Fig. 11(f), the RAR becomes zero at critical Оө c вүҲ 0.24вҲҶ, and SNR R n1,+ = 1 for Оө c < Оө < вҲҶ as the other two reflections RNR and SAR are always zero. We find that the total probability is equal to 1 for Оө < вҲҶ, while it is less than 1 for Оө > вҲҶ.

figure.head: вҶөFIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. Schematic depiction of the gapped bilayer graphenesuperconductor junction and the possible reflection processes when an electron ei (solid arrow with filled green circle) is incident at an angle Оұ: (a)-(b) О» вүӘ t вҠҘ and (c)-(d) О» вүІ t вҠҘ .The other solid arrows and dotted arrows represent respectively the electron reflections and hole reflections. In (b) and (d), the solid and dotted concentric circles in kx-ky plane denote isoenergy contours for electron and hole excitations, see Eq.(5), and arrows at the horizontal dashed-purple-line are the corresponding group velocities. For the incident electron ei in (a)-(b), the reflected hole is RAR when Оө < EF (dotted blue arrow) and is SAR when Оө > EF (dotted red arrow). The electron-hole conversion for RAR is intraband (only the conduction band) however SAR is due to the interband conversion (conduction band to valence band). In contrast, double Andreev reflections (RAR and SAR) and double normal reflections (RNR and SNR) exist simultaneously for (c)-(d) and the electron-hole conversion is always intraband type.

The other parameters are EF = 0.5вҲҶ and U0 = 10вҲҶ. (d) For EF = 0, the dip near the SC gap moves closure to вҲҶ as the interlayer coupling t вҠҘ increases.

FIG. 7. The normalized conductance G/G0 vs. eV /вҲҶ for different non-zero О»'s in normal region. (a) Conductance behaviour is almost exactly same as obtained in the Fig. 5(a) for U0 = 10вҲҶ. However, it deviates slightly in (b) for U0 = вҲҶ. The other parameters are t вҠҘ = 400вҲҶ and EF = 0.5вҲҶ. The subscript in О» N (S) is added to specify the displacement field О» in N (S) region.

Appendix B: Effect of О» on G/G0 at different EF In order to be consistent with the main text in subsection IV A, we illustrate the effect of the displacement field О» on the normalized conductance G/G 0 for two values of Fermi energy E F = 0.2вҲҶ and 0.8вҲҶ to study the contribution of RAR and SAR in subgap region eV < вҲҶ. The results are shown in Figs. 9(a)-(b) for the limit t вҠҘ вү« U 0 and in Figs. 9(c)-(d) for the limit t вҠҘ вүӘ U 0 . In both
